blurb: A succulent euphorbia whose green stems kink back and forth in sharp zigzags — hence the name. This one has dropped its leaves and is currently rangy and stressed; the plan is to nurse it back into full leaf.
size: Currently a leafless ~6 in stem, recovering
toxicity: Toxic — irritant sap, harmful to pets

care.light: Bright light — bright indirect on up to full sun — encourages it to leaf out fully and grow sturdy; poor light is a big reason it goes bare and leggy like this one. Harsh, direct summer sun can still scorch the foliage, so ease it into full exposure.
care.water: Soak-and-dry while it's in active growth, letting the mix dry between; keep it drier in winter. It's stressed right now, so err on the drier side — but don't let it bake bone-dry for long, because prolonged drought makes it drop leaves too; a mostly-dry cycle plus good light does the recovery.
care.temperature: Warmth-loving — keep it above ~50°F; cold and wet together will finish it off.
care.dormancy: A warm-season grower; it slows and can drop leaves in cold, low-light spells (which is likely what happened here).
care.feeding: A low-maintenance, undemanding feeder — a dilute feed through the warm growing season once it's actively pushing new leaves is plenty, and none while it rests.
care.potting: Repot in warm weather into fresh gritty mix; stake or prune leggy stems to shape.
care.propagation: Stem cuttings root well — let the cut latex dry and callus first, and wear gloves.
care.bloom: Small red-and-green slipper-shaped flower bracts at the stem tips on happy, mature plants.
care.troubles: Leaf drop and legginess from low light, cold, or drought (its current state); rot from cold-and-wet; mealybugs.
care.toxicity: The milky latex is a genuine irritant — a mild-to-severe contact dermatitis on skin, capable of corneal damage in the eyes, and blistering of the lips and mouth if chewed. Like the rest of its genus it's toxic if eaten. Keep it away from pets and children and handle with gloves.
